title: Global AI Power Mapping
subtitle: Domains, Alliances, and Rule Spaces

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher_unit: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center

alternate_identifiers.description: Policy brief number printed in the PDF

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Global AI Power Mapping Domains, Alliances, and Rule Spaces.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title
Core mapping unit used in the policy brief to analyze global AI power beyond model rankings
Legal, regulatory, institutional, operational, and political conditions under which AI systems are trained, deployed, restricted, exempted, or authorized

description: This policy brief proposes a domain-based framework for mapping global AI power beyond public model rankings, market share, or firm-level competition. It argues that global AI power is distributed through overlapping domains shaped by states, platforms, cloud infrastructure, data regimes, standards, capital networks, language communities, industrial systems, public institutions, and security architectures. The brief identifies seven AI power domains—the U.S.-Backed AI Domain, China-Backed AI Domain, EU Regulatory Domain, India Service and Language Domain, Gulf Capital and Compute Nodes, Russian Sovereign Security AI Domain, and Global South AI Application Zones—and highlights overlap zones where AI infrastructure, standards, localization, data governance, and geopolitical alignment may be contested.
abstract: Global AI power should not be measured only by market share, user numbers, company valuations, or public model rankings. These indicators capture visible competition, but they do not fully explain the deeper distribution of AI capability, influence, and control. A more accurate map of global AI power must account for the institutional, infrastructural, financial, regulatory, linguistic, industrial, and security systems through which AI is developed and deployed. This policy brief proposes a domain-based approach to global AI power mapping. Rather than treating AI competition as a single leaderboard of models or firms, it maps AI power as a set of overlapping domains of influence. These domains combine technological capability with institutional authority, infrastructure control, regulatory power, capital allocation, language-cultural reach, industrial embedding, and security integration. The brief identifies seven proposed AI power domains: the U.S.-Backed AI Domain, China-Backed AI Domain, EU Regulatory Domain, India Service and Language Domain, Gulf Capital and Compute Nodes, Russian Sovereign Security AI Domain, and Global South AI Application Zones. The central argument is that global AI power is not a single market-share contest. It is a layered and networked structure shaped by states, platforms, data regimes, infrastructure, capital, standards, languages, industrial systems, and security institutions.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.framework_name: Global AI Power Domain Mapping Framework
methodology.source_basis: Open-source policy analysis and conceptual mapping based on AI capability, compute, governance, development, infrastructure, regulatory, public-sector, and strategic assessments, including prior EPINOVA frameworks on system-level AI power and AI capability stratification.
methodology.case_context: Global distribution of AI power across domains, alliances, infrastructure systems, regulatory regimes, capital networks, language-cultural zones, public-sector adoption, and security architectures.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is how to map global AI power beyond public model rankings by identifying the domains and rule spaces through which AI capability becomes scalable, governable, deployable, and strategically consequential.
central_claims: Model rankings are insufficient for mapping global AI power.
AI power is distributed through domains, not only firms.
The United States and China remain the two leading AI capability domains, but they do not exhaust the global AI power structure.
The European Union may exercise AI power primarily through rules rather than frontier model dominance.
India, the Gulf states, Russia, and the Global South occupy distinct positions in the emerging AI order.
Overlap zones will become key arenas of AI competition across infrastructure, standards, localization, data governance, cloud deployment, capital, and geopolitical alignment.

analytical_lenses.name: Domain-Based AI Power Mapping
analytical_lenses.description: Maps AI power as overlapping spheres of influence rather than as a single ranking of countries, firms, or models.

analytical_lenses.name: Rule-Space Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Examines legal, regulatory, institutional, operational, and political conditions that shape AI training, deployment, restriction, exemption, or authorization.

analytical_lenses.name: Infrastructure and Compute Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses how cloud systems, data centers, semiconductors, energy, and compute availability shape AI capability and strategic influence.

analytical_lenses.name: Regulatory Power Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates how standards, risk classifications, compliance obligations, privacy rules, and market access conditions shape global AI behavior.

analytical_lenses.name: Overlap-Zone Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Identifies regions where multiple AI power domains interact, compete, or coexist across cloud deployment, infrastructure, localization, standards, and alignment.

analytical_lenses.name: Socio-Technical Systems Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Treats AI systems as embedded in organizational, legal, institutional, infrastructural, and security contexts rather than isolated model artifacts.

dimensions.name: U.S.-Backed AI Domain
dimensions.observed_pattern: Dense ecosystem of frontier model companies, global platform firms, cloud providers, semiconductor design, venture capital, research universities, and alliance networks.
dimensions.interpretation: Combines frontier model development with global software platforms, advanced chip design, cloud infrastructure, deep capital markets, and allied-market integration.

dimensions.name: China-Backed AI Domain
dimensions.observed_pattern: Industrial applications, infrastructure-scale deployment, domestic data ecosystems, state-market coordination, platform systems, manufacturing integration, and governance mechanisms.
dimensions.interpretation: Its strategic significance lies in embedding AI into production systems, infrastructure networks, public governance, and industrial policy rather than public chatbot rankings alone.

dimensions.name: EU Regulatory Domain
dimensions.observed_pattern: Standards, compliance systems, privacy governance, market access rules, risk regulation, institutional oversight, and normative influence.
dimensions.interpretation: Exercises AI power primarily by shaping rules for design, deployment, auditing, market access, and governance.

dimensions.name: India Service and Language Domain
dimensions.observed_pattern: Software services, technical labor, English-language capability, multilingual markets, public digital infrastructure, and global service integration.
dimensions.interpretation: Influence comes from deployment, adaptation, maintenance, localization, and integration across business services, public administration, education, finance, and multilingual environments.

dimensions.name: Gulf Capital and Compute Nodes
dimensions.observed_pattern: Sovereign capital, energy resources, data-center investment, compute infrastructure, AI financing, and strategic positioning between major AI blocs.
dimensions.interpretation: Functions as a capital-and-compute node rather than a conventional frontier-model bloc.

dimensions.name: Russian Sovereign Security AI Domain
dimensions.observed_pattern: National security priorities, sovereign technology systems, strategic autonomy, cyber capabilities, military applications, and state-centered control over sensitive AI deployment.
dimensions.interpretation: AI influence is concentrated in closed systems, security institutions, strategic autonomy efforts, cyber, military, intelligence, and information operations.

dimensions.name: Global South AI Application Zones
dimensions.observed_pattern: Infrastructure needs, public services, local-language adaptation, development finance, digital inclusion, platform competition, and governance experimentation.
dimensions.interpretation: Central arena for AI deployment, localization, public-sector adoption, standards diffusion, and infrastructure competition rather than frontier model dominance.

dimensions.name: Overlap Zones
dimensions.observed_pattern: Southeast Asia, the Middle East, Africa, Latin America, Central Asia, Eastern Europe, and Eurasian borderlands interact across domains.
dimensions.interpretation: Key arenas where infrastructure, standards, cloud deployment, capital, localization, data governance, and geopolitical alignment may be contested.

key_findings: Public model performance matters, but it does not by itself determine strategic AI influence.
Global AI power is distributed through domains involving states, platforms, cloud systems, data regimes, standards bodies, security alliances, industrial systems, and capital networks.

The United States and China remain the two leading AI capability domains, but other actors exercise influence through regulation, services, language reach, capital, compute, security systems, and deployment markets.
The European Union’s AI power is primarily rule-based, operating through regulatory standards, compliance requirements, privacy governance, risk classification, and market access conditions.
India’s AI power lies in services, technical labor, multilingual deployment, digital public infrastructure, and integration capacity.
The Gulf states are emerging as capital-and-compute nodes rather than as a conventional model-centered AI bloc.
Russia’s AI influence is likely to be concentrated in sovereign-security, cyber, military, intelligence, and state-controlled applications.
Global South application zones will be central to deployment, localization, public-sector adoption, standards diffusion, and infrastructure competition.

Overlap zones will become key arenas of AI competition because AI power depends on financing, cloud availability, standards, language support, public-sector partnerships, security ties, infrastructure investment, and local political acceptance.
policy_recommendations: Assess AI power through domains rather than only through model rankings, market share, or company valuations.

Map the systems that make AI capability scalable, deployable, governable, and consequential, including compute, cloud infrastructure, standards, data regimes, public-sector adoption, and security integration.
Treat regulatory power, capital nodes, service hubs, sovereign-security systems, and Global South application markets as distinct sources of AI influence.
Monitor overlap zones where AI infrastructure, cloud deployment, localization, standards, data governance, and geopolitical alignment are contested.
Develop socio-technical AI governance assessments that combine technical performance with organizational context, infrastructure, public-sector adoption, and institutional accountability.
Avoid one-dimensional rankings that conflate frontier model dominance with overall global AI power.

watchpoints.indicator: Expansion of cloud and data-center contracts in Southeast Asia, the Middle East, Africa, Latin America, Central Asia, Eastern Europe, and Eurasian borderlands
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks whether overlap zones are shifting toward U.S.-backed, China-backed, Gulf-financed, EU-regulated, or mixed AI infrastructure patterns.

watchpoints.indicator: EU AI Act implementation, GPAI code compliance, and extraterritorial corporate adaptation
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses the strength of EU regulatory-domain influence beyond frontier model ownership.

watchpoints.indicator: Gulf AI data-center buildout, sovereign AI investment, compute hosting, and cross-bloc partnerships
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Measures the strategic role of Gulf capital and compute nodes.

watchpoints.indicator: IndiaAI Mission implementation, public digital infrastructure integration, multilingual model deployment, and global AI services growth
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Evaluates the expansion of India’s service and language domain.

watchpoints.indicator: Global South adoption of AI systems in education, public services, digital identity, health, finance, agriculture, and government administration
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks how application markets, localization, and public-sector deployment shape AI power distribution.

watchpoints.indicator: Security-domain AI integration in military, cyber, intelligence, information operations, and sovereign-control systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses less-visible forms of AI power not captured by public market metrics.

figures.title: Global AI Power Domains and Major Overlap Zones
figures.description: Conceptual map illustrating seven proposed AI power domains and major overlap zones where AI infrastructure, standards, cloud deployment, capital, localization, data governance, and geopolitical alignment may be contested.
figures.source: Author’s conceptual mapping based on the AI power domain framework developed in this policy brief, with reference to prior EPINOVA AI power and capability stratification frameworks and AI capability, compute, governance, development, and infrastructure assessments.
figures.note: Domains are approximate analytical categories rather than fixed geopolitical blocs. Country placement reflects primary analytical classification and does not imply exclusive alignment, formal alliance status, or uniform national policy.

funding.statement: No external funding information is provided in the PDF.

software.statement: No software artifact is specified in the PDF.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
